sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 133, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Agent at rows 18-23, trail retracted. Blocks at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40. Need to understand trail-block interaction.
- Plan: Extend trail with RIGHT, then check if trail touches blocks. Try ACTION7 to extend trail at top position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 132 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 2.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After ACTION7:
- Agent at rows 12-17, trail extended (c at rows 12-13, cols 17-46)
- Blocks moved UP! Red at rows 19-22, blue at rows 25-28, green at rows 31-34
- Previously blocks were at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40
So ACTION7 not only moves the agent and extends the trail, but also moves the blocks UP!
When the agent moves UP (from 18-23 to 12-17), the blocks move UP by 6 rows (from 25-28 to 19-22, etc.).
This is a key insight! ACTION7 moves the agent UP and moves the blocks UP.
Regular DOWN moves the agent DOWN and moves the blocks DOWN.
Regular UP moves the agent UP without moving the blocks.
So the blocks move when:
1. ACTION7 (toggles agent position and moves blocks)
2. Regular DOWN (moves agent and blocks DOWN)
The blocks don't move when:
1. Regular UP (moves agent UP without moving blocks)
2. Regular LEFT/RIGHT (moves agent horizontally)
Now, the trail is at rows 12-13, and the red block is at rows 19-22. The trail is 6 rows above the red block.
If I move the agent DOWN, the blocks move DOWN. So the trail and blocks move together.
Wait, but the trail is at the agent's row. When the agent moves DOWN, the trail moves DOWN.
And the blocks also move DOWN when the agent moves DOWN.
So the trail and blocks maintain their relative positions.
Hmm, but the trail is at rows 12-13 and the red block is at rows 19-22. The trail is 6 rows above the red block.
If I move the agent DOWN by 6 rows (to rows 18-23), the trail moves to rows 18-19 (or 19-22), and the blocks move to rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40.
So the trail is still 6 rows above the red block.
Wait, but the trail is at rows 12-13 when the agent is at rows 12-17. The trail is at the top 2 rows of the agent.
When the agent is at rows 18-23, the trail is at rows 19-22 (the middle 4 rows of the agent? Or the top 4 rows?).
Actually, looking at the segmentation, when the agent is at rows 18-23, the trail is retracted (no c object).
When the agent is at rows 12-17, the trail is extended (c at rows 12-13).
So the trail is only extended when the agent is at rows 12-17 (top position).
When the agent is at rows 18-23 (bottom position), the trail is retracted.
This means I can't have the trail extended while the agent is at the bottom position.
And the trail is at rows 12-13, which is above all the blocks (red at 19-22, blue at 25-28, green at 31-34).
So the trail never touches the blocks. The trail is always above the blocks.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is not about the trail touching the blocks.
Maybe the goal is about the order of the blocks. The palette shows: red, green, blue.
The panel shows: red, blue, green.
The goal might be to rearrange the blocks to match the palette order.
But how? The blocks move UP/DOWN together, maintaining their relative order.
Unless... there's a way to swap the blocks.
Wait, let me look at the G (dark gray) objects between the agent and the blocks.
There are G objects at rows 22-25, 28-31, 34-37. These are vertical bars between the agent and the blocks.
Maybe these G objects are related to the block order.
Actually, looking at the segmentation:
- G pixels=8 rows=22-25 cols=13-14 (vertical bar)
- G pixels=8 rows=28-31 cols=13-14 (vertical bar)
- G pixels=8 rows=34-37 cols=13-14 (vertical bar)
These are vertical bars at cols 13-14, between the agent (cols 11-16) and the blocks (cols 42-45).
They're at rows 22-25, 28-31, 34-37, which are between the blocks.
Hmm, these G bars might be separators or connectors between the blocks.
Let me try a different approach. Let me try clicking on the G bars to see if they do something.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: ACTION7 moves agent UP to rows 12-17, extends trail, and moves blocks UP. Blocks now at rows 19-22, 25-28, 31-34. Trail at rows 12-13, above all blocks.
Plan: Try clicking on the G separator bars between agent and blocks to see if they interact.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the G separator bars
# G bars at cols 13-14, rows 22-25, 28-31, 34-37
# Try clicking on the first G bar (rows 22-25)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 23, 'col': 13}])
print(f"Click G bar 1: gameplay_changed={result.get('gameplay_changed')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 5 and n['pixels'] < 100:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min, r_max = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c_min, c_max = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        if r_min < 55:
            print(f"  {n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={r_min}-{r_max} cols={c_min}-{c_max}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
